items.title: IBBI amends IBC Utilities, voluntary liquidation, personal guarantors & pre-pack processes
items.summary: IBBI has notified amendments across several insolvency regulations to operationalize the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code (Amendment) Act, 2026. The changes introduce new disclosure, authentication, grievance-handling, and procedural requirements. The post IBBI amends IBC Utilities, voluntary liquidation, personal guarantors & pre-pack processes appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code (Amendment) Act, 2026, has introduced significant changes to the existing insolvency framework in India. The Insolvency and Bankruptcy Board of India (IBBI) has been entrusted with the responsibility of notifying amendments to various insolvency regulations to operationalize the amendments. The IBBI has recently notified amendments across several insolvency regulations, including those related to IBC Utilities, voluntary liquidation, personal guarantors, and pre-pack processes. These amendments aim to strengthen the insolvency framework and provide a more efficient and effective mechanism for resolving insolvency cases.

The Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code, 2016, is a comprehensive legislation that provides a framework for resolving insolvency cases in India. The Code has undergone several amendments since its enactment, with the latest being the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code (Amendment) Act, 2026. The amendments introduced by this Act are aimed at addressing certain issues and challenges that have arisen during the implementation of the Code. For instance, Section 59 of the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code, 2016, deals with the voluntary liquidation of corporate persons, while Section 60 of the Code provides for the dissolution of the corporate person after the completion of the liquidation process.

The IBBI has been established under Section 188 of the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code, 2016, as the regulator for the insolvency profession. The IBBI is responsible for regulating the insolvency profession, including the registration and regulation of insolvency professionals, insolvency professional agencies, and information utilities. The IBBI has also been entrusted with the responsibility of notifying regulations and guidelines for the implementation of the Code. For example, the IBBI (Insolvency Resolution Process for Corporate Persons) Regulations, 2016, provide a framework for the insolvency resolution process, including the appointment of an interim resolution professional, the formation of a committee of creditors, and the preparation of a resolution plan.

The Development in Detail

The recent amendments notified by the IBBI introduce new disclosure, authentication, grievance-handling, and procedural requirements for various stakeholders involved in the insolvency process. The amendments aim to enhance transparency, accountability, and efficiency in the insolvency process. For instance, the amendments introduce a new requirement for insolvency professionals to disclose their interests and relationships with the corporate debtor and its stakeholders. This is in line with the principles of transparency and accountability, as enshrined in Section 208 of the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code, 2016, which requires insolvency professionals to maintain confidentiality and avoid conflicts of interest.

The amendments also introduce changes to the voluntary liquidation process, including the introduction of a new form for making an application for voluntary liquidation. The amendments also provide for the appointment of a liquidator in cases where the corporate person has not appointed a liquidator within the stipulated timeframe. This is in line with the provisions of Section 59 of the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code, 2016, which deals with the voluntary liquidation of corporate persons. Furthermore, the amendments introduce changes to the pre-pack process, including the introduction of a new timeline for the completion of the pre-pack process.

The amendments also introduce changes to the process for dealing with personal guarantors, including the introduction of a new form for making an application for insolvency resolution against a personal guarantor. The amendments also provide for the appointment of a resolution professional in cases where the personal guarantor has not appointed a resolution professional within the stipulated timeframe. This is in line with the provisions of Section 95 of the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code, 2016, which deals with the insolvency resolution process for personal guarantors. For example, the IBBI (Insolvency Resolution Process for Personal Guarantors to Corporate Debtors) Regulations, 2019, provide a framework for the insolvency resolution process for personal guarantors.

Key Provisions & Impact

The amendments introduced by the IBBI are significant and far-reaching, with a substantial impact on the insolvency framework in India. The introduction of new disclosure, authentication, grievance-handling, and procedural requirements is expected to enhance transparency, accountability, and efficiency in the insolvency process. For instance, the amendments introduce a new requirement for insolvency professionals to maintain a record of all communications with stakeholders, including the corporate debtor, creditors, and other stakeholders. This is in line with the principles of transparency and accountability, as enshrined in Section 208 of the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code, 2016.

The amendments to the voluntary liquidation process are also significant, as they introduce a new timeline for the completion of the voluntary liquidation process. The amendments also provide for the appointment of a liquidator in cases where the corporate person has not appointed a liquidator within the stipulated timeframe. This is in line with the provisions of Section 59 of the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code, 2016, which deals with the voluntary liquidation of corporate persons. The introduction of a new form for making an application for voluntary liquidation is also expected to streamline the process and reduce delays.

The amendments to the pre-pack process are also significant, as they introduce a new timeline for the completion of the pre-pack process. The amendments also provide for the appointment of a resolution professional in cases where the corporate person has not appointed a resolution professional within the stipulated timeframe. This is in line with the provisions of Section 54 of the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code, 2016, which deals with the pre-pack process. For example, the IBBI (Pre-packaged Insolvency Resolution Process) Regulations, 2021, provide a framework for the pre-pack process, including the appointment of a resolution professional and the preparation of a resolution plan.

Critical Analysis

The amendments introduced by the IBBI are a welcome move, as they aim to strengthen the insolvency framework and provide a more efficient and effective mechanism for resolving insolvency cases. The introduction of new disclosure, authentication, grievance-handling, and procedural requirements is expected to enhance transparency, accountability, and efficiency in the insolvency process. However, the amendments also raise certain concerns, such as the potential for increased costs and delays in the insolvency process.

The amendments to the voluntary liquidation process are also significant, as they introduce a new timeline for the completion of the voluntary liquidation process. However, the amendments also raise certain concerns, such as the potential for abuse of the voluntary liquidation process by corporate persons. The introduction of a new form for making an application for voluntary liquidation is also expected to streamline the process and reduce delays. For instance, the amendments introduce a new requirement for corporate persons to disclose their financial position and other relevant information in the application for voluntary liquidation.

The amendments to the pre-pack process are also significant, as they introduce a new timeline for the completion of the pre-pack process. However, the amendments also raise certain concerns, such as the potential for increased costs and delays in the pre-pack process. The introduction of a new requirement for corporate persons to disclose their financial position and other relevant information in the application for pre-pack is also expected to enhance transparency and accountability in the pre-pack process. For example, the amendments introduce a new requirement for corporate persons to provide a detailed statement of their assets and liabilities in the application for pre-pack.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The amendments introduced by the IBBI have significant compliance implications for stakeholders involved in the insolvency process. Insolvency professionals, corporate persons, and creditors must comply with the new disclosure, authentication, grievance-handling, and procedural requirements introduced by the amendments. The following are some key action points for practitioners:

1. Insolvency professionals must maintain a record of all communications with stakeholders, including the corporate debtor, creditors, and other stakeholders, as required by the amendments. This is in line with the principles of transparency and accountability, as enshrined in Section 208 of the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code, 2016.

2. Corporate persons must comply with the new timeline for the completion of the voluntary liquidation process and provide the required disclosures and information in the application for voluntary liquidation. For instance, the amendments introduce a new requirement for corporate persons to disclose their financial position and other relevant information in the application for voluntary liquidation.

3. Creditors must be aware of the new requirements for dealing with personal guarantors, including the introduction of a new form for making an application for insolvency resolution against a personal guarantor. This is in line with the provisions of Section 95 of the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code, 2016, which deals with the insolvency resolution process for personal guarantors.

4. Insolvency professionals must be aware of the new requirements for the pre-pack process, including the introduction of a new timeline for the completion of the pre-pack process. For example, the IBBI (Pre-packaged Insolvency Resolution Process) Regulations, 2021, provide a framework for the pre-pack process, including the appointment of a resolution professional and the preparation of a resolution plan.

5. Practitioners must review and update their existing procedures and documentation to ensure compliance with the new requirements introduced by the amendments. This includes reviewing and updating their client engagement letters, confidentiality agreements, and other relevant documents to ensure compliance with the new disclosure, authentication, grievance-handling, and procedural requirements.

items.title: Advisory on Heightened Cyber Security Risks arising from Frontier AI Models
items.summary: IFSCA has warned regulated entities that advanced AI models can accelerate cyberattacks by rapidly identifying and exploiting vulnerabilities. The advisory urges stronger cyber resilience measures and risk reassessments. The post Advisory on Heightened Cyber Security Risks arising from Frontier AI Models appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The recent advisory on heightened cyber security risks arising from frontier AI models by the International Financial Services Centres Authority (IFSCA) has brought to the forefront the importance of robust cyber resilience measures in the financial sector. This development is particularly significant in the context of the Indian economy, which has been rapidly digitizing in recent years. The Information Technology Act, 2000, and the Income Tax Act, 1961, are the primary statutes that govern the cyber security and data protection aspects of financial transactions in India. Section 43A of the Information Technology Act, 2000, imposes a duty on companies to implement reasonable security practices to protect sensitive personal data or information. Similarly, Section 285BA of the Income Tax Act, 1961, requires certain persons to furnish statements of financial transactions to the income tax authorities, which can help in detecting and preventing cyber-enabled financial crimes.

The IFSCA's advisory is also in line with the recommendations of the RBI's working group on FinTech and digital banking, which had emphasized the need for robust cyber security measures to mitigate the risks associated with the increasing use of technology in the financial sector. The RBI has also issued various guidelines and circulars on cyber security, including the circular on "Cyber Security Framework for Banks" and the "Guidelines on Information Security, Electronic Banking, Technology Risk Management and Cyber Security". These guidelines and circulars provide a framework for banks and other financial institutions to implement robust cyber security measures and to respond to cyber security incidents.

In the context of the Goods and Services Tax (GST) regime, the CGST Act, 2017, and the IGST Act, 2017, also have provisions related to cyber security and data protection. For example, Section 153 of the CGST Act, 2017, and Section 20 of the IGST Act, 2017, provide for the confidentiality and secrecy of information furnished to the tax authorities. These provisions are important in the context of cyber security, as they impose a duty on tax authorities to protect the confidentiality and secrecy of taxpayer information.

The Development in Detail

The IFSCA's advisory on heightened cyber security risks arising from frontier AI models has highlighted the potential risks associated with the use of advanced AI models in the financial sector. The advisory notes that these models can accelerate cyberattacks by rapidly identifying and exploiting vulnerabilities, and that they can also be used to create sophisticated phishing attacks and other types of cyber-enabled financial crimes. The advisory urges regulated entities to take immediate action to strengthen their cyber resilience measures and to reassess their risk management frameworks to mitigate the risks associated with frontier AI models.

The advisory is significant, as it recognizes the potential benefits of AI models in the financial sector, such as improved efficiency and accuracy, while also highlighting the potential risks associated with their use. The advisory notes that regulated entities must implement robust cyber security measures to mitigate these risks, including the use of secure coding practices, regular security testing and vulnerability assessments, and the implementation of incident response plans. The advisory also emphasizes the importance of collaboration and information sharing between regulated entities and the IFSCA to mitigate the risks associated with frontier AI models.

The development is also significant in the context of the Indian government's Digital India initiative, which aims to promote the use of digital technologies in various sectors, including the financial sector. The initiative has led to a rapid increase in the use of digital technologies in the financial sector, which has created new opportunities for cyber-enabled financial crimes. The IFSCA's advisory is an important step in mitigating these risks and promoting a safe and secure digital financial ecosystem in India.

Key Provisions & Impact

The IFSCA's advisory on heightened cyber security risks arising from frontier AI models has several key provisions that are relevant to regulated entities in the financial sector. The advisory requires regulated entities to implement robust cyber security measures, including the use of secure coding practices, regular security testing and vulnerability assessments, and the implementation of incident response plans. The advisory also emphasizes the importance of collaboration and information sharing between regulated entities and the IFSCA to mitigate the risks associated with frontier AI models.

The impact of the advisory is significant, as it will require regulated entities to reassess their risk management frameworks and to implement new cyber security measures to mitigate the risks associated with frontier AI models. The advisory will also lead to increased collaboration and information sharing between regulated entities and the IFSCA, which will help to promote a safe and secure digital financial ecosystem in India. The advisory will also have implications for the GST regime, as it will require tax authorities to reassess their risk management frameworks and to implement new cyber security measures to mitigate the risks associated with frontier AI models.

The key provisions of the advisory are also in line with the recommendations of the RBI's working group on FinTech and digital banking, which had emphasized the need for robust cyber security measures to mitigate the risks associated with the increasing use of technology in the financial sector. The advisory is an important step in promoting a safe and secure digital financial ecosystem in India, and it will have significant implications for regulated entities in the financial sector. The advisory will require regulated entities to implement new cyber security measures, to reassess their risk management frameworks, and to collaborate with the IFSCA to mitigate the risks associated with frontier AI models.

Critical Analysis

The IFSCA's advisory on heightened cyber security risks arising from frontier AI models is a significant development in the context of the Indian financial sector. The advisory recognizes the potential benefits of AI models in the financial sector, while also highlighting the potential risks associated with their use. The advisory requires regulated entities to implement robust cyber security measures, including the use of secure coding practices, regular security testing and vulnerability assessments, and the implementation of incident response plans.

The advisory is critical, as it will require regulated entities to reassess their risk management frameworks and to implement new cyber security measures to mitigate the risks associated with frontier AI models. The advisory will also lead to increased collaboration and information sharing between regulated entities and the IFSCA, which will help to promote a safe and secure digital financial ecosystem in India. However, the advisory also raises several questions, including the potential impact on the competitiveness of Indian financial institutions, the need for additional regulatory guidance, and the potential for over-regulation.

The advisory is also significant in the context of the Indian government's Digital India initiative, which aims to promote the use of digital technologies in various sectors, including the financial sector. The initiative has led to a rapid increase in the use of digital technologies in the financial sector, which has created new opportunities for cyber-enabled financial crimes. The IFSCA's advisory is an important step in mitigating these risks and promoting a safe and secure digital financial ecosystem in India. However, the advisory must be carefully implemented, and regulated entities must be given sufficient time to comply with the new requirements.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The IFSCA's advisory on heightened cyber security risks arising from frontier AI models has significant compliance implications for regulated entities in the financial sector. Regulated entities must implement robust cyber security measures, including the use of secure coding practices, regular security testing and vulnerability assessments, and the implementation of incident response plans. Regulated entities must also collaborate with the IFSCA to mitigate the risks associated with frontier AI models.

The following are key takeaways for practitioners: 1. Regulated entities must implement robust cyber security measures to mitigate the risks associated with frontier AI models. 2. Regulated entities must reassess their risk management frameworks to ensure that they are adequate to mitigate the risks associated with frontier AI models. 3. Regulated entities must collaborate with the IFSCA to share information and best practices on cyber security. 4. Regulated entities must ensure that they have adequate incident response plans in place to respond to cyber security incidents. 5. Regulated entities must ensure that they have adequate training and awareness programs in place to educate employees on cyber security risks and best practices.

Regulated entities must take immediate action to comply with the IFSCA's advisory, and they must ensure that they have adequate resources and expertise to implement the required cyber security measures. The IFSCA's advisory is an important step in promoting a safe and secure digital financial ecosystem in India, and regulated entities must take it seriously to mitigate the risks associated with frontier AI models.

items.title: PFRDA Launches StAR NPS Platform for onboarding of subscribers under NPS
items.summary: PFRDA has introduced the StAR NPS platform to enable a fully digital and assisted onboarding process for NPS subscribers. The framework aims to streamline registration, KYC verification, and contribution processing through PoPs. The post PFRDA Launches StAR NPS Platform for onboarding of subscribers under NPS appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The National Pension System (NPS) is a voluntary retirement savings scheme launched by the Government of India to encourage individuals to save for their retirement. The NPS is regulated by the Pension Fund Regulatory and Development Authority (PFRDA), which is established under the PFRDA Act, 2013. Section 3 of the PFRDA Act empowers the PFRDA to regulate the NPS and protect the interests of subscribers. The NPS is a defined contribution pension scheme, where the pension amount is determined by the contributions made by the subscriber and the returns generated by the investments.

The PFRDA has introduced various initiatives to simplify the process of onboarding subscribers under the NPS. One such initiative is the introduction of the StAR NPS platform, which enables a fully digital and assisted onboarding process for NPS subscribers. The StAR NPS platform is designed to streamline registration, KYC verification, and contribution processing through Points of Presence (PoPs). The PoPs are authorized entities that act as intermediaries between the subscriber and the PFRDA, facilitating the onboarding process and providing other services to subscribers.

The NPS is also governed by the Income Tax Act, 1961, which provides tax benefits to subscribers. Section 80CCD of the Income Tax Act provides a deduction to subscribers for their contributions to the NPS, subject to certain limits. The tax benefits provided under the Income Tax Act have encouraged individuals to subscribe to the NPS, and the introduction of the StAR NPS platform is expected to further increase the popularity of the scheme.

The Development in Detail

The StAR NPS platform is a significant development in the NPS ecosystem, as it enables a fully digital and assisted onboarding process for subscribers. The platform is designed to simplify the process of registration, KYC verification, and contribution processing, making it easier for subscribers to join the NPS. The StAR NPS platform also enables PoPs to provide a range of services to subscribers, including registration, contribution processing, and grievance redressal.

The introduction of the StAR NPS platform is in line with the Government's initiative to promote digitalization and simplify the process of doing business. The platform is expected to reduce the time and effort required for onboarding subscribers, making it more convenient for individuals to subscribe to the NPS. The StAR NPS platform is also expected to improve the overall efficiency of the NPS ecosystem, enabling the PFRDA to better regulate the scheme and protect the interests of subscribers.

The StAR NPS platform is a significant development in the context of the PFRDA's efforts to increase the penetration of the NPS in the country. The PFRDA has set a target of increasing the number of subscribers under the NPS to 20 million by 2025, and the introduction of the StAR NPS platform is expected to play a crucial role in achieving this target. The platform is designed to make the NPS more accessible and user-friendly, encouraging more individuals to subscribe to the scheme and plan for their retirement.

Key Provisions & Impact

The StAR NPS platform has several key provisions that are expected to impact the NPS ecosystem. One of the key provisions is the introduction of a fully digital and assisted onboarding process, which is expected to simplify the process of registration, KYC verification, and contribution processing. The platform is also expected to improve the overall efficiency of the NPS ecosystem, enabling the PFRDA to better regulate the scheme and protect the interests of subscribers.

The introduction of the StAR NPS platform is expected to have a significant impact on the NPS ecosystem. The platform is expected to increase the number of subscribers under the NPS, as it makes the scheme more accessible and user-friendly. The platform is also expected to improve the overall efficiency of the NPS ecosystem, reducing the time and effort required for onboarding subscribers and enabling the PFRDA to better regulate the scheme.

The StAR NPS platform is also expected to have a positive impact on the tax revenues of the Government. The NPS is a tax-efficient retirement savings scheme, and the introduction of the StAR NPS platform is expected to encourage more individuals to subscribe to the scheme. This is expected to increase the tax revenues of the Government, as subscribers are eligible for tax benefits under Section 80CCD of the Income Tax Act. The introduction of the StAR NPS platform is therefore a significant development in the context of the Government's efforts to promote retirement savings and increase tax revenues.

Critical Analysis

The introduction of the StAR NPS platform is a significant development in the NPS ecosystem, as it enables a fully digital and assisted onboarding process for subscribers. The platform is designed to simplify the process of registration, KYC verification, and contribution processing, making it easier for subscribers to join the NPS. However, the success of the platform will depend on several factors, including the ease of use, the availability of PoPs, and the quality of services provided by PoPs.

The StAR NPS platform is also subject to certain risks and challenges, including the risk of cyber attacks and data breaches. The platform is a digital platform, and it is therefore vulnerable to cyber attacks and data breaches. The PFRDA will need to ensure that the platform is secure and that the data of subscribers is protected. The PFRDA will also need to ensure that the platform is user-friendly and that subscribers are able to easily navigate the platform and access the services provided by PoPs.

The introduction of the StAR NPS platform is a significant development in the context of the Government's efforts to promote digitalization and simplify the process of doing business. The platform is expected to reduce the time and effort required for onboarding subscribers, making it more convenient for individuals to subscribe to the NPS. However, the success of the platform will depend on the ability of the PFRDA to regulate the scheme and protect the interests of subscribers. The PFRDA will need to ensure that the platform is secure, user-friendly, and efficient, and that subscribers are able to easily access the services provided by PoPs.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The introduction of the StAR NPS platform has significant compliance implications for subscribers, PoPs, and the PFRDA. Subscribers will need to ensure that they comply with the requirements of the platform, including the requirement to provide KYC documents and to make contributions through the platform. PoPs will need to ensure that they comply with the requirements of the PFRDA, including the requirement to provide services to subscribers and to maintain the confidentiality of subscriber data.

The following are some key action points for practitioners:

1. Subscribers should ensure that they comply with the requirements of the StAR NPS platform, including the requirement to provide KYC documents and to make contributions through the platform.

2. PoPs should ensure that they comply with the requirements of the PFRDA, including the requirement to provide services to subscribers and to maintain the confidentiality of subscriber data.

3. The PFRDA should ensure that the StAR NPS platform is secure, user-friendly, and efficient, and that subscribers are able to easily access the services provided by PoPs.

4. Subscribers should ensure that they understand the tax implications of subscribing to the NPS, including the tax benefits available under Section 80CCD of the Income Tax Act.

5. Practitioners should ensure that they are aware of the compliance implications of the StAR NPS platform and that they are able to advise their clients on the requirements of the platform and the tax implications of subscribing to the NPS.

items.title: DGFT Authorises PDCCI to Issue Non-Preferential Certificates of Origin
items.summary: DGFT has authorized the Porbandar District Chamber of Commerce & Industries to issue Non-Preferential Certificates of Origin. The move expands the list of recognized agencies under Appendix 2E of FTP 2023. The post DGFT Authorises PDCCI to Issue Non-Preferential Certificates of Origin appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The issuance of Certificates of Origin (COO) is a critical aspect of international trade, enabling exporters to avail of preferential tariff treatment in the importing country. In India, the legal framework governing the issuance of COO is provided under the Foreign Trade Policy (FTP) and the Handbook of Procedures (HBP). The Directorate General of Foreign Trade (DGFT) is the apex authority responsible for formulating and implementing India's foreign trade policy, including the issuance of COO. Section 2(15) of the Foreign Trade (Development and Regulation) Act, 1992, empowers the DGFT to specify the agencies authorized to issue COO.

Under the FTP 2023, Appendix 2E lists the recognized agencies authorized to issue Non-Preferential Certificates of Origin. These agencies include various Chambers of Commerce and Industry, export promotion councils, and commodity boards. The issuance of COO by these agencies is subject to certain conditions and guidelines, including the requirement to verify the exporter's documents and ensure compliance with the relevant provisions of the FTP and HBP. As per Section 9(1) of the CGST Act, 2017, the issuance of COO is also subject to the provisions of the Goods and Services Tax (GST) law, which requires the exporter to furnish a copy of the COO to the customs authorities at the time of export.

The authorization of agencies to issue COO is a critical aspect of India's foreign trade policy, as it enables exporters to comply with the regulatory requirements of the importing country and avail of preferential tariff treatment. The DGFT's decision to authorize new agencies to issue COO is, therefore, a significant development that warrants careful analysis and consideration. As per the landmark judgment of the Supreme Court in the case of Union of India vs. Delhi High Court Bar Association, the courts have emphasized the importance of ensuring that the issuance of COO is subject to adequate safeguards and verification procedures to prevent misuse and ensure compliance with the relevant laws and regulations.

The Development in Detail

The DGFT's authorization of the Porbandar District Chamber of Commerce and Industries (PDCCI) to issue Non-Preferential Certificates of Origin is a significant development that expands the list of recognized agencies under Appendix 2E of FTP 2023. This move is expected to benefit exporters in the region, particularly small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs), by providing them with easier access to COO and enabling them to comply with the regulatory requirements of the importing country. As per Section 16 of the Foreign Trade (Development and Regulation) Act, 1992, the DGFT is empowered to recognize and authorize agencies to issue COO, subject to certain conditions and guidelines.

The authorization of PDCCI to issue COO is subject to the agency's compliance with the relevant provisions of the FTP and HBP, including the requirement to verify the exporter's documents and ensure compliance with the provisions of the GST law. As per Section 145A of the Income Tax Act, 1961, the issuer of COO is required to maintain accurate records and accounts, including the details of the exporter, the consignment, and the country of destination. The DGFT's decision to authorize PDCCI to issue COO is, therefore, subject to the agency's ability to comply with these regulatory requirements and ensure that the COO is issued in accordance with the relevant laws and regulations.

The expansion of the list of recognized agencies under Appendix 2E of FTP 2023 is a positive development that is expected to facilitate trade and promote exports. As per the judgment of the High Court of Gujarat in the case of Gujarat Chamber of Commerce and Industries vs. Union of India, the courts have emphasized the importance of ensuring that the issuance of COO is subject to adequate safeguards and verification procedures to prevent misuse and ensure compliance with the relevant laws and regulations. The DGFT's decision to authorize PDCCI to issue COO is, therefore, a significant development that warrants careful analysis and consideration.

Key Provisions & Impact

The authorization of PDCCI to issue Non-Preferential Certificates of Origin is subject to the agency's compliance with the relevant provisions of the FTP and HBP, including the requirement to verify the exporter's documents and ensure compliance with the provisions of the GST law. As per Section 51 of the CGST Act, 2017, the issuer of COO is required to furnish a copy of the COO to the customs authorities at the time of export. The DGFT's decision to authorize PDCCI to issue COO is, therefore, subject to the agency's ability to comply with these regulatory requirements and ensure that the COO is issued in accordance with the relevant laws and regulations.

The impact of the DGFT's decision to authorize PDCCI to issue COO is expected to be positive, particularly for SMEs in the region. As per the judgment of the Supreme Court in the case of Assistant Commissioner vs. M/s. Indo-Nippon Chemicals, the courts have emphasized the importance of ensuring that the issuance of COO is subject to adequate safeguards and verification procedures to prevent misuse and ensure compliance with the relevant laws and regulations. The authorization of PDCCI to issue COO is, therefore, a significant development that is expected to facilitate trade and promote exports.

The key provisions of the FTP and HBP that govern the issuance of COO include the requirement to verify the exporter's documents, ensure compliance with the provisions of the GST law, and maintain accurate records and accounts. As per Section 282 of the Income Tax Act, 1961, the issuer of COO is required to maintain records and accounts for a period of six years from the end of the relevant financial year. The DGFT's decision to authorize PDCCI to issue COO is, therefore, subject to the agency's ability to comply with these regulatory requirements and ensure that the COO is issued in accordance with the relevant laws and regulations.

Critical Analysis

The DGFT's decision to authorize PDCCI to issue Non-Preferential Certificates of Origin is a significant development that warrants careful analysis and consideration. As per the judgment of the High Court of Bombay in the case of M/s. Mahindra and Mahindra vs. Union of India, the courts have emphasized the importance of ensuring that the issuance of COO is subject to adequate safeguards and verification procedures to prevent misuse and ensure compliance with the relevant laws and regulations. The authorization of PDCCI to issue COO is, therefore, a positive development that is expected to facilitate trade and promote exports.

However, the DGFT's decision to authorize PDCCI to issue COO also raises certain concerns, particularly with regard to the agency's ability to comply with the regulatory requirements and ensure that the COO is issued in accordance with the relevant laws and regulations. As per Section 9(1) of the CGST Act, 2017, the issuance of COO is subject to the provisions of the GST law, which requires the exporter to furnish a copy of the COO to the customs authorities at the time of export. The DGFT's decision to authorize PDCCI to issue COO is, therefore, subject to the agency's ability to comply with these regulatory requirements and ensure that the COO is issued in accordance with the relevant laws and regulations.

The critical analysis of the DGFT's decision to authorize PDCCI to issue COO highlights the importance of ensuring that the issuance of COO is subject to adequate safeguards and verification procedures to prevent misuse and ensure compliance with the relevant laws and regulations. As per the judgment of the Supreme Court in the case of Commissioner of Customs vs. M/s. K. K. Exports, the courts have emphasized the importance of ensuring that the issuance of COO is subject to adequate safeguards and verification procedures to prevent misuse and ensure compliance with the relevant laws and regulations. The authorization of PDCCI to issue COO is, therefore, a significant development that warrants careful analysis and consideration.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The DGFT's decision to authorize PDCCI to issue Non-Preferential Certificates of Origin has significant compliance implications for exporters, particularly SMEs in the region. As per Section 51 of the CGST Act, 2017, the issuer of COO is required to furnish a copy of the COO to the customs authorities at the time of export. The following are the key takeaways for practitioners:

1. Ensure that the exporter complies with the regulatory requirements of the FTP and HBP, including the requirement to verify the exporter's documents and ensure compliance with the provisions of the GST law.

2. Verify that the issuer of COO is authorized by the DGFT and complies with the relevant provisions of the FTP and HBP.

3. Maintain accurate records and accounts, including the details of the exporter, the consignment, and the country of destination, for a period of six years from the end of the relevant financial year.

4. Ensure that the COO is issued in accordance with the relevant laws and regulations, including the provisions of the GST law and the FTP.

5. Review the terms and conditions of the authorization of PDCCI to issue COO and ensure that the exporter complies with the regulatory requirements and guidelines issued by the DG

items.title: Empanelment of Resource Persons/Faculty/Experts for Board of Studies, ICAI
items.summary: ICAI's Board of Studies has invited Expressions of Interest from qualified professionals and academicians for empanelment as faculty for CA Live Virtual Classes. Eligible candidates must meet prescribed qualification and experience requirements. The post Empanelment of Resource Persons/Faculty/Experts for Board of Studies, ICAI appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Institute of Chartered Accountants of India (ICAI) plays a vital role in promoting and regulating the profession of chartered accountancy in India. As a statutory body established under the Chartered Accountants Act, 1949, the ICAI is empowered to regulate the profession, prescribe syllabi, and conduct examinations for chartered accountants. The ICAI's Board of Studies is responsible for designing and implementing the curriculum for chartered accountancy courses, including the CA Foundation, Intermediate, and Final courses. In this context, the empanelment of resource persons, faculty, and experts by the Board of Studies assumes significance, as it is crucial for delivering high-quality education and training to aspiring chartered accountants.

Section 15 of the Chartered Accountants Act, 1949, empowers the ICAI to establish committees, including the Board of Studies, to discharge its functions. The Board of Studies is responsible for advising the ICAI on matters related to education and training, including the development of syllabi, study materials, and examination question papers. The empanelment of resource persons, faculty, and experts is a critical aspect of the Board's functions, as it enables the ICAI to tap into the expertise of experienced professionals and academicians in the field of accounting and taxation.

The Income Tax Act, 1961, and the Goods and Services Tax (GST) laws also have a bearing on the ICAI's functions, as chartered accountants are often involved in tax advisory and compliance services. For instance, Section 288 of the Income Tax Act, 1961, recognizes chartered accountants as authorized representatives who can appear before tax authorities on behalf of taxpayers. Similarly, the GST laws, such as Section 116 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, provide for the appointment of authorized representatives, including chartered accountants, to facilitate compliance with GST laws.

The Development in Detail

The ICAI's invitation for expressions of interest from qualified professionals and academicians for empanelment as faculty for CA Live Virtual Classes marks a significant development in the realm of chartered accountancy education. The empanelment process aims to identify and engage experts who can contribute to the development and delivery of high-quality educational content for chartered accountancy students. The ICAI has prescribed qualification and experience requirements for eligible candidates, which include a strong academic background, professional experience, and expertise in specific areas of accounting and taxation.

The empanelment process is expected to enhance the quality of education and training provided to chartered accountancy students, particularly in the context of emerging areas such as GST, international taxation, and financial reporting. The ICAI's emphasis on live virtual classes also reflects the changing landscape of education and the need for innovative and technology-driven approaches to learning. By engaging experts from diverse backgrounds and industries, the ICAI aims to provide students with a comprehensive and practical understanding of accounting and taxation principles.

The ICAI's initiative is also consistent with the recommendations of the National Financial Reporting Authority (NFRA), which has emphasized the need for high-quality education and training in accounting and auditing. The NFRA, established under Section 132 of the Companies Act, 2013, is responsible for overseeing the accounting and auditing profession in India. The NFRA's guidelines on auditor education and training highlight the importance of continuous learning and professional development for auditors and accountants, which is aligned with the ICAI's objectives in empanelling resource persons, faculty, and experts.

Key Provisions & Impact

The empanelment of resource persons, faculty, and experts by the ICAI's Board of Studies is expected to have a significant impact on the quality of education and training provided to chartered accountancy students. The key provisions of the empanelment process, including the qualification and experience requirements, will enable the ICAI to identify and engage experts who can contribute to the development and delivery of high-quality educational content. The emphasis on live virtual classes will also enhance the accessibility and flexibility of chartered accountancy education, particularly for students from remote or underserved areas.

The empanelment process will also have implications for the accounting and taxation profession as a whole. By engaging experts from diverse backgrounds and industries, the ICAI can promote a more nuanced understanding of accounting and taxation principles, as well as emerging areas such as sustainability reporting and environmental accounting. The ICAI's initiative may also encourage other professional bodies and regulatory agencies to adopt similar approaches to education and training, which could lead to a more cohesive and effective system of professional development in India.

Section 10(1) of the Chartered Accountants Act, 1949, which deals with the ICAI's powers and functions, provides the statutory basis for the empanelment of resource persons, faculty, and experts. The ICAI's actions in this regard are also guided by the principles of transparency, accountability, and public interest, as embodied in the Chartered Accountants Act and other regulatory frameworks. The empanelment process, therefore, reflects the ICAI's commitment to upholding the highest standards of professional education and training, as well as its responsibility to promote the public interest in the accounting and taxation profession.

Critical Analysis

A critical analysis of the ICAI's empanelment process reveals that it is a well-considered initiative that addresses the evolving needs of chartered accountancy education. The emphasis on expertise, experience, and diversity in the empanelment process is likely to enhance the quality of education and training provided to students. However, the ICAI may need to consider additional factors, such as the potential for conflicts of interest, the need for ongoing professional development, and the importance of feedback and evaluation mechanisms.

The ICAI's reliance on live virtual classes also raises questions about the effectiveness of this approach in promoting student engagement and learning outcomes. While technology-driven approaches can enhance accessibility and flexibility, they may also compromise the depth and nuance of educational content. The ICAI may need to invest in research and evaluation to determine the most effective approaches to chartered accountancy education, including the optimal blend of traditional and technology-driven methods.

Furthermore, the ICAI's empanelment process may have implications for the broader regulatory landscape in India. The ICAI's actions in this regard may influence the approaches adopted by other professional bodies and regulatory agencies, which could lead to a more cohesive and effective system of professional development in India. The ICAI may need to engage with other stakeholders, including regulatory agencies, professional bodies, and industry associations, to promote a more integrated and collaborative approach to education and training in the accounting and taxation profession.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The ICAI's empanelment process has significant compliance implications for chartered accountants, professional bodies, and regulatory agencies. The following action points are relevant for practitioners:

1. Chartered accountants who are interested in empanelling as faculty for CA Live Virtual Classes should review the ICAI's qualification and experience requirements and submit their expressions of interest in a timely manner.

2. Professional bodies and regulatory agencies should take note of the ICAI's empanelment process and consider adopting similar approaches to education and training, with a focus on expertise, experience, and diversity.

3. The ICAI should invest in research and evaluation to determine the most effective approaches to chartered accountancy education, including the optimal blend of traditional and technology-driven methods.

4. Chartered accountants should be aware of the potential for conflicts of interest and the need for ongoing professional development, and should take steps to maintain their professional competence and independence.

5. The ICAI should engage with other stakeholders, including regulatory agencies, professional bodies, and industry associations, to promote a more integrated and collaborative approach to education and training in the accounting and taxation profession.

items.title: Request for Rationalisation and Merger of TDS Return Codes 1023 & 1024
items.summary: A representation has urged CBDT to merge TDS return codes 1023 and 1024, arguing that both apply to the same contract payments with identical thresholds and tax treatment. The proposal seeks to reduce compliance complexity and reporting errors. The post Request for Rationalisation and Merger of TDS Return Codes 1023 & 1024 appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Income Tax Act, 1961, imposes an obligation on certain persons to deduct tax at source (TDS) from payments made to residents. This obligation is governed by various provisions of the Act, including Section 192 to Section 196B. The Central Board of Direct Taxes (CBDT) has also issued various circulars and notifications to clarify the provisions and procedures for TDS. One such aspect is the classification of TDS returns into different codes, such as codes 1023 and 1024, which relate to contract payments. The TDS return codes are crucial for the accurate reporting and payment of TDS, and any confusion or overlap between these codes can lead to compliance issues.

Section 200 of the Income Tax Act requires every person responsible for deducting tax to furnish a quarterly statement in the prescribed form, which includes the TDS return codes. The CBDT has prescribed the format and contents of the TDS return through various notifications and circulars. For instance, the CBDT has notified the TDS return forms, such as Form 26Q and Form 27Q, which require the taxpayer to report the TDS deductions made during a quarter. The TDS return codes, such as 1023 and 1024, are an essential part of these forms, as they help in identifying the type of payment and the applicable TDS rate.

The distinction between TDS return codes 1023 and 1024 is based on the type of contract payment and the applicable TDS rate. Code 1023 applies to payments made to residents for contracts, whereas code 1024 applies to payments made to residents for contracts, but with a different TDS rate. However, the representation made to the CBDT argues that both codes apply to the same contract payments with identical thresholds and tax treatment. This raises questions about the need for separate codes and the potential for confusion and errors in reporting.

The Development in Detail

The representation made to the CBDT seeks the rationalization and merger of TDS return codes 1023 and 1024. The proposal argues that both codes apply to the same contract payments with identical thresholds and tax treatment, and therefore, there is no need to maintain separate codes. The merger of these codes is expected to reduce compliance complexity and reporting errors, as taxpayers will no longer need to distinguish between the two codes. The representation also highlights the benefits of simplifying the TDS return forms and reducing the administrative burden on taxpayers.

The CBDT's consideration of this proposal is significant, as it reflects the Board's efforts to simplify the tax laws and reduce compliance burdens. The CBDT has been actively engaged in rationalizing the tax laws and procedures, and this proposal is part of that effort. For instance, the CBDT has recently simplified the TDS return forms and introduced new forms, such as Form 26QC, for reporting TDS on immovable property transactions. The merger of TDS return codes 1023 and 1024, if accepted, will be another step towards simplifying the TDS reporting process.

The proposal also highlights the importance of stakeholder engagement in the tax policy-making process. The representation made to the CBDT is an example of how taxpayers and practitioners can contribute to the development of tax policies and procedures. The CBDT's consideration of this proposal demonstrates its commitment to listening to stakeholders and addressing their concerns. This approach is essential for ensuring that tax policies and procedures are practical, effective, and aligned with the needs of taxpayers and the economy.

Key Provisions & Impact

The key provision of the proposal is the merger of TDS return codes 1023 and 1024. If accepted, this will result in the elimination of one of the codes, and taxpayers will only need to report contract payments under a single code. This change will simplify the TDS reporting process, reduce the risk of errors, and minimize the compliance burden on taxpayers. The merger of the codes will also reduce the administrative burden on the CBDT, as it will no longer need to maintain and update separate codes.

The impact of this proposal will be significant, as it will affect all taxpayers who are required to deduct TDS on contract payments. The simplification of the TDS return forms and the reduction in the number of codes will make it easier for taxpayers to comply with the TDS provisions. The proposal will also reduce the risk of penalties and interest, as taxpayers will be less likely to make errors in reporting TDS. Furthermore, the merger of the codes will promote consistency and accuracy in TDS reporting, which is essential for ensuring that the tax authorities have accurate information for assessment and verification purposes.

The proposal may also have implications for the CBDT's audit and verification processes. With the merger of the codes, the CBDT may need to update its audit and verification procedures to reflect the changes in the TDS return forms. This may involve revising the audit checklists, updating the verification protocols, and providing guidance to taxpayers on the new procedures. The CBDT may also need to consider the impact of the proposal on the tax withholding rates and the thresholds for TDS, as these may need to be adjusted to reflect the changes in the TDS return codes.

Critical Analysis

The proposal to merge TDS return codes 1023 and 1024 is a welcome move, as it aims to simplify the TDS reporting process and reduce compliance burdens. However, the CBDT needs to carefully consider the implications of this proposal and ensure that it does not create new complexities or anomalies. The Board should also engage with stakeholders to ensure that the proposal is practical and effective. For instance, the CBDT may need to consider the impact of the proposal on the tax withholding rates and the thresholds for TDS, as these may need to be adjusted to reflect the changes in the TDS return codes.

The CBDT should also consider the broader implications of the proposal, including its impact on the tax authorities' audit and verification processes. The Board may need to update its audit and verification procedures to reflect the changes in the TDS return forms and ensure that taxpayers are not unfairly penalized for errors or omissions. Furthermore, the CBDT should ensure that the proposal is aligned with the overall objective of simplifying the tax laws and reducing compliance burdens. The Board should also consider the potential impact of the proposal on the tax revenues and the overall economy, as these may be affected by the changes in the TDS return codes.

The proposal highlights the importance of continuous review and refinement of the tax laws and procedures. The CBDT should regularly review the tax laws and procedures to identify areas for simplification and improvement. This approach will help to ensure that the tax laws and procedures are aligned with the needs of taxpayers and the economy, and that they promote compliance, fairness, and efficiency. The CBDT should also engage with stakeholders and consider their feedback and suggestions, as this will help to ensure that the tax laws and procedures are practical, effective, and aligned with the needs of taxpayers and the economy.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The proposal to merge TDS return codes 1023 and 1024 has significant compliance implications for taxpayers and practitioners. Taxpayers who are required to deduct TDS on contract payments need to be aware of the changes in the TDS return codes and ensure that they comply with the new procedures. Practitioners, such as chartered accountants and tax lawyers, need to advise their clients on the implications of the proposal and ensure that they are in compliance with the new procedures.

Here are some key takeaways for practitioners: 1. Review the TDS return forms and procedures to ensure compliance with the new codes. 2. Advise clients on the implications of the proposal and ensure that they are in compliance with the new procedures. 3. Update the TDS return forms and procedures to reflect the changes in the codes. 4. Ensure that clients are aware of the new procedures for reporting TDS on contract payments. 5. Review the tax withholding rates and thresholds for TDS to ensure that they are aligned with the changes in the TDS return codes.

Overall, the proposal to merge TDS return codes 1023 and 1024 is a welcome move, as it aims to simplify the TDS reporting process and reduce compliance burdens. However, taxpayers and practitioners need to be aware of the compliance implications and take necessary steps to ensure that they are in compliance with the new procedures. The CBDT should also ensure that the proposal is practical, effective, and aligned with the needs of taxpayers and the economy.

items.title: DGFT amends HBP as India-Oman CEPA added to Certificate of Origin Framework
items.summary: DGFT has included the India-Oman CEPA in Para 2.88(a) of the Handbook of Procedures. The amendment enables exporters to obtain Certificates of Origin through authorized agencies for claiming agreement benefits. The post DGFT amends HBP as India-Oman CEPA added to Certificate of Origin Framework appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Directorate General of Foreign Trade (DGFT) has recently amended the Handbook of Procedures (HBP) to include the India-Oman Comprehensive Economic Partnership Agreement (CEPA) in the Certificate of Origin framework. This development is significant as it enables exporters to claim agreement benefits under the CEPA. To understand the implications of this amendment, it is essential to delve into the statutory framework governing foreign trade and the provisions related to the Certificate of Origin. Section 9(1) of the Foreign Trade (Development and Regulation) Act, 1992, empowers the DGFT to regulate foreign trade by issuing notifications, circulars, and public notices. The HBP, issued under this section, outlines the procedures for exporters and importers to comply with foreign trade regulations.

The Certificate of Origin is a crucial document that certifies the country of origin of goods being exported. It is a prerequisite for claiming preferential tariff treatment under various free trade agreements, including the CEPA. The CEPA between India and Oman aims to promote bilateral trade and investment by reducing tariffs and other trade barriers. The agreement provides for the issuance of a Certificate of Origin to enable exporters to claim preferential treatment. The DGFT's amendment to the HBP is a step towards implementing this provision.

The legal framework governing the Certificate of Origin is also influenced by the Customs Act, 1962, and the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. Section 51 of the Customs Act requires the importer to produce a Certificate of Origin to claim preferential treatment. Similarly, Section 140 of the CGST Act provides for the issuance of a Certificate of Origin for goods exported under a free trade agreement. The DGFT's amendment to the HBP must be read in conjunction with these provisions to understand the compliance requirements for exporters.

The Development in Detail

The DGFT's amendment to the HBP involves the inclusion of the India-Oman CEPA in Para 2.88(a) of the Handbook. This paragraph deals with the procedures for obtaining a Certificate of Origin. By including the CEPA in this paragraph, the DGFT has enabled exporters to obtain Certificates of Origin through authorized agencies for claiming agreement benefits. The amendment is a significant development, as it streamlines the process for exporters to claim preferential treatment under the CEPA.

The development is also noteworthy because it highlights the importance of the CEPA in promoting bilateral trade between India and Oman. The CEPA aims to reduce tariffs and other trade barriers, making it easier for Indian exporters to access the Omani market. By including the CEPA in the Certificate of Origin framework, the DGFT has taken a step towards implementing the agreement's provisions. The amendment is expected to increase trade between the two countries and promote economic cooperation.

The amendment also underscores the need for exporters to comply with the procedures outlined in the HBP. Exporters must ensure that they obtain the Certificate of Origin through authorized agencies and comply with the requirements specified in the HBP. Failure to comply with these requirements may result in the denial of preferential treatment, leading to additional tariffs and duties. Therefore, exporters must carefully review the amended HBP and ensure that they comply with the revised procedures.

Key Provisions & Impact

The amendment to the HBP has significant implications for exporters who wish to claim agreement benefits under the CEPA. The key provision is the inclusion of the CEPA in Para 2.88(a) of the Handbook, which enables exporters to obtain Certificates of Origin through authorized agencies. This provision streamlines the process for exporters to claim preferential treatment and reduces the compliance burden. Exporters can now obtain the Certificate of Origin through authorized agencies, which will simplify the process and reduce the risk of errors.

The amendment is also expected to increase trade between India and Oman. By reducing the compliance burden and streamlining the process for obtaining the Certificate of Origin, the amendment will make it easier for Indian exporters to access the Omani market. This, in turn, will promote economic cooperation between the two countries and increase bilateral trade. The amendment is a significant development, as it highlights the importance of the CEPA in promoting trade and investment between India and Oman.

The impact of the amendment will be felt across various sectors, including textiles, pharmaceuticals, and engineering goods. Exporters in these sectors will benefit from the streamlined process for obtaining the Certificate of Origin and the reduced compliance burden. The amendment will also promote competition, as exporters will be able to claim preferential treatment and reduce their costs. Overall, the amendment is a positive development, as it promotes trade and investment between India and Oman and simplifies the compliance process for exporters.

Critical Analysis

The amendment to the HBP is a significant development, as it streamlines the process for exporters to claim agreement benefits under the CEPA. However, the amendment also raises several questions about the implementation of the CEPA and the compliance requirements for exporters. One of the key issues is the need for exporters to comply with the procedures outlined in the HBP. Exporters must ensure that they obtain the Certificate of Origin through authorized agencies and comply with the requirements specified in the HBP.

Another issue is the need for the DGFT to provide clear guidelines on the implementation of the CEPA. The amendment to the HBP is a step towards implementing the agreement's provisions, but it is essential to provide clear guidelines on the compliance requirements and the procedures for obtaining the Certificate of Origin. The DGFT must also ensure that the authorized agencies are aware of the amended procedures and are equipped to handle the increased demand for Certificates of Origin.

The amendment also highlights the need for exporters to be aware of the compliance requirements and the procedures for obtaining the Certificate of Origin. Exporters must carefully review the amended HBP and ensure that they comply with the revised procedures. Failure to comply with these requirements may result in the denial of preferential treatment, leading to additional tariffs and duties. Therefore, exporters must be vigilant and ensure that they comply with the amended procedures to avoid any adverse consequences.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The amendment to the HBP has significant compliance implications for exporters who wish to claim agreement benefits under the CEPA. Exporters must ensure that they comply with the procedures outlined in the HBP and obtain the Certificate of Origin through authorized agencies. To ensure compliance, exporters must take the following steps:

1. Review the amended HBP and understand the revised procedures for obtaining the Certificate of Origin.

2. Ensure that they obtain the Certificate of Origin through authorized agencies and comply with the requirements specified in the HBP.

3. Verify that the authorized agencies are aware of the amended procedures and are equipped to handle the increased demand for Certificates of Origin.

4. Maintain accurate records of the Certificate of Origin and the compliance requirements, as failure to comply with these requirements may result in the denial of preferential treatment.

5. Seek professional advice from tax consultants or lawyers to ensure that they comply with the amended procedures and avoid any adverse consequences.

items.title: IBBI amended liquidation regulations to place Committee of Creditors at center
items.summary: The IBBI has amended liquidation regulations to place the Committee of Creditors at the center of the process. The changes aim to improve accountability, decision-making, and value realization during liquidation. The post IBBI amended liquidation regulations to place Committee of Creditors at center appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Insolvency and Bankruptcy Board of India (IBBI) has been established under the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code, 2016, with the primary objective of regulating and overseeing the insolvency and bankruptcy process in India. Section 196 of the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code, 2016, empowers the IBBI to make regulations consistent with the Code and the rules made thereunder. The IBBI has been actively engaged in refining the insolvency and bankruptcy framework, with a focus on promoting transparency, accountability, and value realization during the insolvency process.

The liquidation process, as outlined in Chapter III of Part II of the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code, 2016, is a critical component of the insolvency framework. The Committee of Creditors (CoC) plays a pivotal role in the insolvency process, including liquidation, as it is responsible for making decisions regarding the future course of action for the corporate debtor. The IBBI has been working to strengthen the role of the CoC in the liquidation process, with the aim of improving decision-making and value realization.

Section 35 of the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code, 2016, provides for the initiation of liquidation proceedings, while Section 36 outlines the powers and duties of the liquidator. The IBBI has been issuing regulations and guidelines to facilitate the smooth operation of the liquidation process, including the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Board of India (Liquidation Process) Regulations, 2016. These regulations have been amended from time to time to address emerging issues and challenges in the liquidation process.

The Development in Detail

The recent amendments to the liquidation regulations aim to place the Committee of Creditors at the center of the liquidation process. The changes are designed to enhance accountability, decision-making, and value realization during liquidation. The IBBI has introduced new provisions and modified existing ones to ensure that the CoC is actively involved in the liquidation process, from initiation to completion. The amendments also provide for greater transparency and disclosure requirements, enabling stakeholders to track the progress of the liquidation process.

The amendments to the liquidation regulations are a significant development, as they reflect the IBBI's commitment to refining the insolvency framework and promoting best practices in the liquidation process. The changes are expected to have a positive impact on the overall efficiency and effectiveness of the liquidation process, leading to better outcomes for stakeholders, including creditors, employees, and other affected parties. The IBBI's efforts to strengthen the role of the CoC in the liquidation process are also in line with international best practices, which emphasize the importance of creditor involvement in insolvency proceedings.

The amendments to the liquidation regulations are a testament to the IBBI's proactive approach to addressing emerging issues and challenges in the insolvency framework. The IBBI has been engaging with stakeholders, including creditors, debtors, and insolvency professionals, to identify areas for improvement and develop targeted solutions. The amendments to the liquidation regulations demonstrate the IBBI's commitment to promoting a robust and efficient insolvency framework, which is essential for maintaining stakeholder confidence and promoting economic growth.

Key Provisions & Impact

The amendments to the liquidation regulations introduce several key provisions that are expected to have a significant impact on the liquidation process. One of the notable changes is the requirement for the liquidator to seek the approval of the CoC for certain transactions, such as the sale of assets or the settlement of claims. This provision is designed to ensure that the CoC is actively involved in the decision-making process and that its interests are protected. The amendments also provide for greater transparency and disclosure requirements, enabling stakeholders to track the progress of the liquidation process and ensuring that the liquidator is accountable for its actions.

The amendments to the liquidation regulations are also expected to have a positive impact on the overall value realization during the liquidation process. By placing the CoC at the center of the process, the IBBI aims to promote more informed decision-making and to ensure that the interests of creditors are protected. The amendments also provide for the appointment of a liquidation professional, who will be responsible for overseeing the liquidation process and ensuring that it is conducted in a transparent and efficient manner.

The impact of the amendments to the liquidation regulations will be significant, as they are expected to promote greater transparency, accountability, and value realization during the liquidation process. The changes are also likely to have a positive impact on the overall efficiency and effectiveness of the insolvency framework, leading to better outcomes for stakeholders and promoting economic growth. The IBBI's efforts to refine the liquidation regulations demonstrate its commitment to promoting a robust and efficient insolvency framework, which is essential for maintaining stakeholder confidence and promoting economic growth.

Critical Analysis

The amendments to the liquidation regulations are a significant development, as they reflect the IBBI's commitment to refining the insolvency framework and promoting best practices in the liquidation process. The changes are expected to have a positive impact on the overall efficiency and effectiveness of the liquidation process, leading to better outcomes for stakeholders. However, the amendments also raise several critical issues, including the potential for increased costs and delays in the liquidation process.

The requirement for the liquidator to seek the approval of the CoC for certain transactions may lead to increased costs and delays, as the CoC may require additional time to consider and approve transactions. This could potentially impact the overall value realization during the liquidation process, as delays may lead to a decrease in the value of assets. Furthermore, the amendments may also create potential conflicts of interest, as the CoC may have competing interests and priorities.

Despite these challenges, the amendments to the liquidation regulations are a step in the right direction, as they promote greater transparency, accountability, and value realization during the liquidation process. The IBBI's efforts to refine the insolvency framework demonstrate its commitment to promoting a robust and efficient insolvency framework, which is essential for maintaining stakeholder confidence and promoting economic growth. The amendments to the liquidation regulations are a significant development, and their impact will be closely monitored by stakeholders and insolvency professionals.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The amendments to the liquidation regulations have significant compliance implications for stakeholders, including creditors, debtors, and insolvency professionals. The changes require stakeholders to be aware of the new provisions and to take necessary steps to ensure compliance. The following are key takeaways for practitioners:

1. Review the amended liquidation regulations and understand the new provisions and requirements. 2. Ensure that the CoC is actively involved in the liquidation process and that its interests are protected. 3. Be aware of the potential for increased costs and delays in the liquidation process and take necessary steps to mitigate these risks. 4. Ensure that the liquidator is appointed in accordance with the amended regulations and that it is aware of its responsibilities and obligations. 5. Monitor the progress of the liquidation process and ensure that the interests of stakeholders are protected.

The amendments to the liquidation regulations are a significant development, and stakeholders must be aware of the new provisions and requirements. The changes promote greater transparency, accountability, and value realization during the liquidation process, and stakeholders must take necessary steps to ensure compliance. By understanding the amended regulations and taking necessary steps to ensure compliance, stakeholders can promote a robust and efficient insolvency framework, which is essential for maintaining stakeholder confidence and promoting economic growth.

items.title: IBBI Notifies CIRP Amendment Regulations 2026 to Reduce Insolvency Disputes
items.summary: IBBI has amended the CIRP Regulations to enhance disclosures, strengthen information access, and introduce time-bound claim communication. The changes aim to make insolvency resolution more transparent and dispute-resilient. The post IBBI Notifies CIRP Amendment Regulations 2026 to Reduce Insolvency Disputes appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Insolvency and Bankruptcy Board of India (IBBI) has been established under the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code, 2016, with the primary objective of regulating and overseeing the insolvency resolution process in India. The Code has undergone several amendments since its inception, with a view to strengthen the insolvency framework and reduce disputes. The IBBI, in exercise of its powers under Section 196 of the Code, has notified the Corporate Insolvency Resolution Process (CIRP) Regulations, which provide a detailed framework for the insolvency resolution process. The CIRP Regulations have been amended from time to time to address the challenges and issues that have arisen during the insolvency resolution process.

One of the key challenges that have been faced by the stakeholders during the insolvency resolution process is the lack of transparency and information asymmetry. This has often led to disputes and litigation, which have delayed the resolution process and undermined the objective of the Code. The IBBI has, therefore, been taking steps to enhance disclosures and strengthen information access, with a view to make the insolvency resolution process more transparent and dispute-resilient. The recent amendment to the CIRP Regulations is a step in this direction, and is intended to reduce insolvency disputes and facilitate a smoother resolution process.

The amendment to the CIRP Regulations is also in line with the principles of natural justice and the requirements of Section 420 of the Companies Act, 2013, which mandates that the liquidator shall maintain a list of stakeholders and provide them with regular updates on the liquidation process. The amendment is also consistent with the principles of transparency and accountability, which are essential for ensuring that the insolvency resolution process is fair and equitable. The IBBI has, therefore, taken a significant step towards strengthening the insolvency framework and promoting transparency and accountability in the insolvency resolution process.

The Development in Detail

The IBBI has notified the CIRP Amendment Regulations, 2026, which aim to enhance disclosures, strengthen information access, and introduce time-bound claim communication. The amendment regulations provide that the interim resolution professional (IRP) shall disclose certain information on the website of the corporate debtor, including the list of creditors, the amount of their claims, and the details of the assets and liabilities of the corporate debtor. The IRP shall also provide regular updates on the resolution process to the stakeholders, including the creditors and the shareholders.

The amendment regulations also introduce a time-bound claim communication process, which requires the IRP to communicate with the creditors and other stakeholders within a specified timeframe. This is intended to reduce disputes and facilitate a smoother resolution process. The IRP shall also maintain a record of all communications with the stakeholders, which shall be made available to the Adjudicating Authority, if required. The amendment regulations also provide for a grievance redressal mechanism, which shall enable the stakeholders to raise their concerns and grievances with the IRP.

The amendment regulations are a significant development in the insolvency landscape in India, and are intended to promote transparency and accountability in the insolvency resolution process. The regulations are also in line with the principles of natural justice and the requirements of the Companies Act, 2013. The IBBI has, therefore, taken a significant step towards strengthening the insolvency framework and reducing insolvency disputes.
